evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns of the digital market

paragraphs.text: We welcome the opportunity to consult on the proposed Digital Fairness Act, and the Commission noting the vulnerability of minors as a key consideration. We concur that gaps in protection still persist, despite consumer protection laws and recent complementary regulatory developments such as the DSA and AI acts to help address the risks and harms associated with specific problems posed by the online environment. Children and young people are particularly vulnerable to dark patterns of the digital market, whilst also making up a large portion of the consumer market. It is for this reason that in the drafting of the proposed Digital Fairness Act, we hope to see childrens rights and child rights based impact assessments at the forefront and prioritised throughout. Childrens rights under the UNCRC are transposed into the digital environment under General Comment 25 under 4 key pillars: 1. That the best interests of the child must always be a primary consideration in all actions affecting children, including those in digital contexts. 2. The right to life, survival, and development underscores the need to ensure childrens safety, well-being, and holistic development as they engage with digital platforms. 3. Non-discrimination requires that all children, regardless of their background or circumstances, have equal access to opportunities and protections in the digital environment, free from exclusion or bias. 4.Respect for the views of the child highlights the importance of recognising children as active participants in decisions affecting their lives, ensuring their voices are heard and valued, particularly in the development and implementation of digital policies. For childrens rights as consumers to be adequately protected, they must be enshrined throughout the Act. Therefore, we recommend that a child right based impact assessment (CRIA) with meaningful child participation is undertaken. This can be kept current and considered an ongoing task as part of the drafting and later of the implementation process, so as to ensure that a childrens rights based assessment is embedded within the regulation. This could also facilitate cohesive implementation of the Act across member states. CRIAs offer a proactive approach to upholding childrens rights by considering them as part of the decision-making process. They can identify rights that may be impacted and facilitate preventative measures to be made to laws or policies to avoid a breach of childrens rights. The children and young people of the EU should be enabled to live in a productive, inclusive and stable environment where all advances in technology, including AI, are integrated through a comprehensive, consistent application of human rights and equality, thus benefitting EU society, economy and culture, while paying particular care to the rights of those who may be at risk of harm or abuse, including protecting the best interests of children. The proposed Digital Fairness Act provides an opportunity to go further than existing Acts such as the DSA and AI Acts, where a failure to prioritise childrens rights in favour of tech advancement is evident. This Act should visibly prioritise the protection of fundamental rights of children. Childrens voices must play an active and meaningful role in the development of this Act if they are to truly benefit from it. Adequate reporting and enforcement mechanisms outside the internal ones of the platforms must be put in place to ensure compliance, so that if a childs right is violated under the DFA, redress will be accessible, swift and age friendly. Whilst complaints mechanism may be complex for everyone, they are particularly complex for children. The act should include capacity for child friendly complaints mechanisms that are easily accessed and legally enforceable. This is particularly relevant for children in Ireland, who may be disadvantaged because of the general absence of class actions.
